summary:
Respiratory Emergencies

Asthma

Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways characterized by episodes of reversible bronchospasm, airway hyperresponsiveness, and mucus production. Acute exacerbations are triggered by allergens, exercise, respiratory infections, cold air, or stress. The hallmark symptom is expiratory wheezing — a high-pitched musical sound heard on auscultation caused by turbulent airflow through narrowed bronchioles.

Key assessment findings in an acute asthma exacerbation include:

  • Wheezing — Expiratory (or both inspiratory/expiratory in severe cases); absence of wheezing in a deteriorating patient is ominous
  • Accessory muscle use — Intercostal, supraclavicular, and substernal retractions; nasal flaring; tripod positioning
  • Prolonged expiratory phase — The expiratory time is significantly longer than inspiration due to air trapping
  • Tachypnea and tachycardia — Compensatory responses to hypoxemia and increased work of breathing
  • Pulsus paradoxus — An exaggerated drop in systolic blood pressure (>10 mmHg) during inspiration
  • Inability to speak in full sentences — Indicates moderate-to-severe obstruction
  • Silent chest — A late and ominous sign indicating severely limited airflow; the patient is too fatigued or obstructed to generate audible wheezing → impending respiratory failure
Silent Chest = Emergency: A patient who was wheezing loudly and is now quiet, drowsy, and has minimal chest movement has decompensated from respiratory distress into respiratory failure. Begin assisted ventilation immediately and expedite transport.

COPD Exacerbation

Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) is a progressive lung disease encompassing emphysema and chronic bronchitis. Patients have chronic dyspnea and may retain carbon dioxide (CO₂), blunting their hypoxic respiratory drive. An acute exacerbation presents with a sudden worsening of baseline dyspnea, increased sputum volume, and/or purulent sputum. Differentiating chronic from acute dyspnea is critical:

FeatureChronic (Baseline)Acute Exacerbation
Dyspnea onsetGradual, progressive over yearsSudden worsening (hours to days)
SputumMinimal, clear/whiteIncreased volume, purulent (yellow/green)
Mental statusBaseline (may be normal)Often altered (confusion, drowsiness from CO₂ retention)
Oxygen requirementStable baseline SpO₂Declining SpO₂ requiring increased FiO₂
Response to bronchodilatorsMinimal changeMay show some improvement

Pneumonia

Pneumonia is an infection of the lung parenchyma caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi. It inflames the alveoli, filling them with fluid and inflammatory cells, impairing gas exchange. The classic presentation includes:

  • Fever and chills — Often high-grade with shaking rigors
  • Crackles (rales) — Heard on auscultation over the affected lung region; caused by fluid in the alveoli
  • Productive cough — Purulent (yellow/green/rust-colored) sputum
  • Pleuritic chest pain — Sharp pain worsened by deep breathing or coughing (if inflammation extends to the pleura)
  • Tachypnea, tachycardia, and hypoxemia — Dependent on severity
  • Dullness to percussion — Over consolidated lung tissue
  • Bronchial breath sounds — Heard over consolidated areas

EMS provider management: place in position of comfort, titrate oxygen to SpO₂ ≥94%, assist ventilations if deteriorating, and transport. Be alert for sepsis criteria (altered mental status, fever, hypotension, tachycardia).

Pulmonary Embolism

A pulmonary embolism (PE) occurs when a thrombus (usually from the deep veins of the legs) travels to the pulmonary circulation, obstructing blood flow and causing ventilation-perfusion mismatch. PE can be rapidly fatal if massive. Classic presentation:

  • Sudden onset dyspnea — Often out of proportion to exam findings
  • Pleuritic chest pain — Sharp, worsens with inspiration
  • Tachypnea and tachycardia — Common compensatory responses
  • Hemoptysis — Coughing up blood (may occur with pulmonary infarction)
  • Hypoxemia — Low SpO₂ despite supplemental oxygen
  • Risk factors — Include prolonged immobility (long flights, bed rest), recent surgery, pregnancy, cancer, oral contraceptive use, obesity, smoking, and history of DVT
  • Signs of DVT — Unilateral leg swelling, warmth, erythema, and calf tenderness

Saddle embolus — a massive PE lodged at the bifurcation of the pulmonary artery — can cause abrupt cardiovascular collapse, syncope, and cardiac arrest (PEA). EMS provider management: high-flow oxygen, position of comfort, rapid transport, and be prepared to perform CPR.

Hyperventilation Syndrome

Hyperventilation syndrome (HVS) is a pattern of rapid, deep breathing driven by anxiety or panic, not by a physiological need to increase oxygenation. The increased respiratory rate blows off excessive CO₂, leading to respiratory alkalosis, which causes:

  • Perioral and distal extremity paresthesias (tingling around the mouth and in the fingers)
  • Carpo-pedal spasm (tetany — painful cramping of the hands and feet)
  • Lightheadedness and dizziness
  • Chest tightness or palpitations

Critical: Hyperventilation syndrome is a DIAGNOSIS OF EXCLUSION. You must first rule out life-threatening causes of tachypnea including PE, asthma exacerbation, DKA, pneumonia, and hypoxia. NEVER use the "paper bag" rebreathing technique — it is dangerous and has caused deaths from hypoxia. Management: calm reassurance, encourage the patient to slow their breathing, rule out organic causes, and transport if symptoms persist or diagnosis is uncertain.

EMS Provider Management of Respiratory Emergencies

  • Position of comfort: Sitting upright (tripod position) optimizes diaphragmatic excursion and air movement. Avoid supine positioning in patients with respiratory distress.
  • Oxygen titration: General patients: titrate to SpO₂ ≥94%. Known COPD patients: titrate to 88-92% to avoid blunting hypoxic drive. Use nasal cannula, simple mask, or non-rebreather as needed.
  • Bronchodilator administration: EMS providers may administer a nebulized bronchodilator (e.g., albuterol 2.5 mg in 3 mL normal saline via small-volume nebulizer with O₂ at 6-8 L/min). Monitor for tachycardia and tremor. Repeat doses per protocol.
  • CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure): Used for pulmonary edema and COPD exacerbation. CPAP splints open alveoli, improves oxygenation, reduces work of breathing, and can decrease the need for intubation. Contraindications: apnea, hypotension, facial trauma, vomiting, altered mental status.
  • Assisted ventilation: If the patient deteriorates into respiratory failure (falling SpO₂, decreasing respiratory rate, declining mental status), begin BVM ventilation with supplemental oxygen and prepare for rapid transport.

quiz.q: A 22-year-old female with asthma is in respiratory distress. You hear faint expiratory wheezing, but her breath sounds are much quieter than they were 5 minutes ago. She is drowsy and using accessory muscles. What does this change MOST likely indicate?
options: The asthma exacerbation is resolving with treatment
quiz.explanation: A silent chest with declining mental status signals severe airflow limitation and impending respiratory failure, not improvement.

quiz.q: A 68-year-old male with known COPD presents with acute onset of severe dyspnea, sharp right-sided chest pain, and an SpO₂ of 84% on room air. He recently returned from a 12-hour flight. What condition should you suspect?
quiz.explanation: Sudden dyspnea + pleuritic chest pain + hypoxemia + recent prolonged immobility (flight) is classic for pulmonary embolism.

quiz.q: [HARD] A 72-year-old female with COPD presents with acute dyspnea, SpO₂ 85%, and a respiratory rate of 28. Your partner applies a non-rebreather at 15 L/min. Five minutes later, the patient becomes drowsy and her respiratory rate drops to 10. What is the MOST likely cause?
options: She is improving and able to rest now
Over-oxygenation has blunted her hypoxic respiratory drive
She has developed a pneumothorax from the oxygen mask
quiz.explanation: In chronic CO₂ retainers, excessive O₂ can blunt the hypoxic drive, leading to hypoventilation and CO₂ narcosis. Titrate O₂ to 88-92%.

flashcards.question: What are the classic signs of an acute asthma exacerbation?
flashcards.answer: Expiratory wheezing, accessory muscle use (retractions, nasal flaring, tripod), prolonged expiratory phase, tachypnea, tachycardia, inability to speak in full sentences, and pulsus paradoxus.

flashcards.question: What does a 'silent chest' in an asthma patient indicate?
flashcards.answer: A late and ominous sign of severe airflow limitation — the patient is too fatigued or obstructed to generate audible wheezing. It indicates impending respiratory failure requiring immediate assisted ventilation.

flashcards.question: How do you differentiate chronic vs acute dyspnea in a COPD patient?
flashcards.answer: Chronic dyspnea is gradual and stable at baseline. Acute exacerbation involves sudden worsening of dyspnea, increased sputum volume, purulent sputum (yellow/green), altered mental status (CO₂ retention), and declining SpO₂.

flashcards.question: What are the classic signs of pneumonia?
flashcards.answer: Fever and chills, crackles (rales) on auscultation, productive cough with purulent sputum, pleuritic chest pain, tachypnea, hypoxemia, dullness to percussion, and bronchial breath sounds.

flashcards.question: What is the classic presentation of a pulmonary embolism?
flashcards.answer: Sudden onset dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachypnea, tachycardia, hypoxemia, and possible hemoptysis. Risk factors include prolonged immobility, recent surgery, pregnancy, cancer, and oral contraceptive use.

flashcards.question: What is hyperventilation syndrome and how should an EMS provider manage it?
flashcards.answer: A pattern of rapid breathing driven by anxiety causing respiratory alkalosis (paresthesias, tetany, lightheadedness). It is a diagnosis of exclusion — rule out PE, asthma, DKA, and hypoxia first. NEVER use paper bag rebreathing. Manage with calm reassurance.

flashcards.question: What is the role of CPAP in prehospital respiratory emergencies?
flashcards.answer: CPAP provides positive pressure to splint open alveoli, improving oxygenation and reducing work of breathing. It is used for pulmonary edema and severe COPD exacerbations. Contraindications include apnea, hypotension, and altered mental status.

flashcards.question: What is the target SpO₂ for oxygen titration in a known COPD patient vs a general patient?
flashcards.answer: General patients: titrate to SpO₂ ≥94%. Known COPD patients (chronic CO₂ retainers): titrate to 88-92% to avoid blunting the hypoxic respiratory drive.

critical.scenario: You are treating a 24-year-old male with a history of asthma who presents with severe respiratory distress. He is sitting in the tripod position, using accessory muscles, and is only able to speak 2-3 words between breaths. You hear loud expiratory wheezing on auscultation. His SpO₂ is 89% on room air, respiratory rate is 32, and heart rate is 124. You administer nebulized albuterol via small-volume nebulizer with oxygen. Five minutes after the treatment, the patient becomes noticeably quieter. His wheezing is now barely audible, his respiratory rate has dropped to 14, and he is increasingly drowsy. His SpO₂ has fallen to 84% despite ongoing oxygen.
critical.question: What does the 'silent chest' with declining mental status indicate, and what is your immediate action?

options.t: The albuterol has resolved the bronchospasm — the patient is improving; continue monitoring
options.f: Incorrect. A silent chest with declining SpO₂ and mental status is the opposite of improvement — it signals decompensation.

options.t: The patient has entered respiratory failure — begin assisted ventilation with BVM, request ALS backup, and expedite transport
options.f: Correct. The silent chest, falling SpO₂, and drowsiness are signs of impending respiratory arrest. Assist ventilations immediately.

critical.explanation: A silent chest with declining mental status is a pre-arrest sign. The patient has exhausted his compensatory mechanisms. Begin BVM ventilation immediately, continue oxygen, and transport rapidly.

critical.scenario: You are dispatched to a 72-year-old female with difficulty breathing. Her family reports she has had a cough with green sputum for 4 days and spiked a fever of 102°F this morning. On exam, she is tachycardic (118 bpm), tachypneic (28/min), and has an SpO₂ of 86% on room air. Her blood pressure is 104/68. Auscultation reveals crackles in the right lower lobe. Her skin is warm and flushed. When asked simple questions, she is confused and unable to tell you what year it is — her family says this is new.
critical.question: Given the fever, productive cough, focal crackles, and new-onset confusion, what condition do you suspect and what is your priority treatment?

options.t: Simple febrile illness — provide cooling and transport non-emergently
options.f: Incorrect. Focal crackles, productive cough, hypoxia, and new confusion suggest pneumonia with possible sepsis.

options.t: Pneumonia with possible sepsis — apply high-flow oxygen, position of comfort, monitor for hypotension, and transport rapidly to the closest emergency department
options.f: Correct. The presentation is classic for pneumonia with sepsis criteria (infection + altered mental status + tachypnea).

critical.explanation: Pneumonia with sepsis requires early recognition, oxygen, and rapid transport. New confusion in a patient with infection is a red flag for sepsis.

critical.scenario: You respond to a 45-year-old female who suddenly developed sharp, stabbing chest pain and difficulty breathing while at rest. She is anxious, tachypneic (32/min), and her SpO₂ is 88% on room air. She has tachycardia at 122 bpm. She reports no cough or fever. Her past medical history includes a DVT two months ago for which she was prescribed blood thinners, but she stopped taking them last week. On exam, you note mild swelling in her left calf.
critical.question: Which condition is most consistent with this presentation and what is the appropriate management?

options.t: Anxiety attack — calm reassurance and reassess
options.f: Incorrect. The hypoxia, tachycardia, and DVT history with medication non-compliance point strongly to pulmonary embolism.

options.t: Pulmonary embolism — high-flow oxygen, position of comfort, rapid transport, and be prepared to manage cardiac arrest
options.f: Correct. Sudden dyspnea + pleuritic pain + hypoxemia + DVT history + recent immobility is classic for PE.

critical.explanation: PE is a high-risk, time-sensitive condition. The patient has multiple risk factors including recent DVT and anticoagulant non-compliance. Provide oxygen and transport rapidly.
